The Food & Drink Hero question

We are looking for those who possess the greatest of qualities and the best working practices. The most outstanding, the
most ethical, the most food fanatical or just simply those who are thought of as the best. Is it because they source the best
ingredients, use the most responsible sources or are they the heart and soul of the community, maybe a person or a business
that has done/created something that is out of the ordinary.

You may wish to include answers to the following questions:
• How do you make North Notts shine?              • What you bring and add to the North Notts area?
• What are your greatest strengths?                 • What aspects of your job make you the North Notts Food & Drink Hero?
• Have you had to overcome challenges to get to where you are today?
Please fill in details in the box 1, 600 words max.

2. Box 2 is for supporting information that you would like to add.
This includes anything that you believe to be relevant to your application including any qualifications, awards or press and social
media that may be of value to your application.

answers.field_label: Box 1 The Food Drink Hero Question 600 Words Max
answers.value:
I am the owner-chef of The Green Fork Bistro, a 32-cover neighbourhood restaurant in Worksop that focuses on seasonal, locally sourced food and low-waste cooking. I am applying for the North Notts Food & Drink Hero award because my work over the last five years has centred on three things: championing local producers, making good food accessible to everyone in our community, and building a genuinely sustainable, ethical business that looks after both people and the planet.

After training and working in Nottingham and Sheffield, I came back to North Notts in 2019 to open The Green Fork on Newcastle Avenue, ten minutes’ walk from where I grew up. From day one, I committed to sourcing at least 80% of our ingredients from within a 40-mile radius. Today, we regularly buy from over 18 local suppliers, including Moss Valley Farm (pork), Welbeck Farm Shop (dairy and charcuterie), Starkey’s Fruit (apples and soft fruit) and a small group of allotment growers in Worksop who supply us with surplus produce through a scheme we set up called "Plot to Plate." I personally visit these suppliers, share their stories on our menus and social media, and invite them to our quarterly producer evenings where customers meet the people behind their food.

Community has always been at the heart of what I do. During the pandemic, when we had to close the restaurant, we converted our kitchen into a community meal hub. Working with Bassetlaw Food Bank and a local church, we cooked and distributed over 7,000 free or pay-what-you-can meals between April 2020 and March 2021, focusing on older residents living alone and families whose children would normally receive free school meals. We used donations, surplus produce, and our own savings to make it happen. Even after reopening, we kept a weekly "Community Table" night: a three-course set menu at cost price, where people can discreetly pay what they can afford. No one is turned away, and regular customers often overpay to cover others.

Sustainability and ethical practice are not marketing buzzwords for us; they are built into how we operate. We reduced our food waste by over 70% in three years by designing root-to-leaf and nose-to-tail menus, preserving gluts from local growers, and training the team to use trimmings creatively (for example, carrot tops become pesto, cauliflower leaves are fermented, and stale bread becomes breadcrumbs and desserts). Any unavoidable food waste is composted in partnership with a local community garden, which in turn grows herbs and salad leaves we buy back. We moved to 100% renewable electricity, switched to refillable cleaning products from a Mansfield-based supplier, and eliminated nearly all single-use plastics in front-of-house.

I am also passionate about skills and opportunities for young people in North Notts. We currently have two apprentices from North Notts College, and I run free quarterly "Intro to Kitchen Skills" workshops for 14–18-year-olds who are curious about hospitality but might not see it as a viable career. Several of our current team members came through these workshops. I make a point of paying real Living Wage as a minimum, offering flexible hours to accommodate childcare and studies, and involving the whole team in menu development so that everyone feels ownership of what we serve.

What makes me proudest is when customers tell us that The Green Fork feels like "their" place – whether they’re celebrating anniversaries, coming in alone with a book, or joining our monthly "Supper & Story" nights where we pair dishes with stories about the farmers and growers who made them possible. I’m not a celebrity chef, and we’re not a flashy restaurant, but I believe that consistent, everyday commitment to good food, fairness, and community is exactly what the North Notts Food & Drink Hero award is about. I would be honoured if our work, and the work of the many local producers we support, could be recognised through this award.

answers.field_label: Box 2 Supporting Information Including Qualifications Awards Press And Social Media
answers.value:
Qualifications and Training:
- Level 3 Professional Cookery, New College Nottingham (2010)
- Level 2 Food Safety & Hygiene for Catering (renewed 2024)
- Level 2 Allergy Awareness in Catering (2023)
- Short course in Sustainable Menu Planning, Sheffield Hallam University (2021)
- First Aid at Work certificate (valid until 2027)

Awards and Recognitions:
- Finalist, Nottinghamshire Best Newcomer Restaurant (2021) – The Green Fork Bistro shortlisted in first year of eligibility.
- Winner, Bassetlaw Green Business Award (2023) – recognised for our low-waste approach, local sourcing, and community meal initiative.
- Highly Commended, North Notts Business Women’s Awards – Entrepreneur of the Year (2022).

Press and Media Coverage:
- "The Little Bistro Making a Big Difference" – feature article in the Worksop Guardian (May 2023) highlighting our community meal programme and partnership with Bassetlaw Food Bank.
- Profile in Nottinghamshire Live food & drink section (October 2022) focusing on our seasonal tasting menu and commitment to local producers.
- Interview on BBC Radio Nottingham (lunchtime show, February 2024) discussing rising food costs, how we keep our community menu affordable, and tips for reducing food waste at home.
- Mentioned in "48 Hours in North Notts" travel piece in a regional lifestyle magazine, recommending The Green Fork Bistro as a must-visit independent restaurant in Worksop.

Social Media and Online Presence:
- Instagram (@greenforkbistro): ~3,800 followers as of February 2026. We post daily behind-the-scenes stories, supplier visits, and short videos showing how to cook simple seasonal dishes at home. Our most-watched reel (over 25k views) is a tutorial on making leek and potato soup using only locally grown ingredients.
- Facebook (The Green Fork Bistro): ~2,400 followers. Used to promote community nights, charity events, and last-minute table availability. We regularly share posts from local producers to amplify their reach.
- Twitter/X (@greenforkbistro): Smaller following (~900), used mainly for industry conversations, sharing sustainability tips, and engaging with local councils and food networks.
- TripAdvisor: Consistently ranked in the top 5 restaurants in Worksop for the past three years, with an average rating of 4.8/5 from over 260 reviews. Common themes in reviews include friendly service, creative use of local ingredients, and welcoming atmosphere for solo diners and families.

Community and Charity Involvement:
- Bassetlaw Food Bank: Ongoing partnership since 2020. We host an annual fundraising dinner where all profits are donated; the 2024 event raised just over £3,200.
- Local schools: I have delivered talks and simple cooking demos at three local primary schools (Worksop Priory, Redlands, and St. Anne’s) to encourage children to try seasonal vegetables and understand where food comes from.
- Community Garden Collaboration: We support a community garden project off Kilton Hill by providing compost, buying their surplus herbs and salad leaves, and hosting their volunteer appreciation lunch once a year free of charge.

Professional Networks:
- Member of the Nottinghamshire Independent Hospitality Network.
- Informal mentor to two new café owners in Retford and Mansfield, offering advice on menu costing, supplier relationships, and staff wellbeing.

All of the above demonstrates that my work at The Green Fork Bistro is not just about running a successful restaurant, but about using food and drink as a vehicle for positive change in North Notts – socially, environmentally, and economically.

persona.background_context:
This is Emma, a 34-year-old owner-chef running The Green Fork Bistro, a cosy 32-cover neighbourhood restaurant on Newcastle Avenue in Worksop, about a ten-minute walk from the estate where she grew up. She shares a small red-brick terrace on Beechwood Terrace with her partner, Daniel Harper, a 36-year-old secondary school science teacher at Outwood Academy Valley, and their slightly neurotic rescue collie-spaniel, Molly. Emma opened The Green Fork in 2019 after years cooking in Nottingham and Sheffield kitchens, and the place has become both her livelihood and her main creative outlet. Her days are long but structured: mornings often start around 7:00 a.m. with a dog walk along the Chesterfield Canal or through The Canch, followed by coffee at the kitchen table while she checks bookings, supplier messages, and overnight social media comments on the bistro’s accounts.

Most weekdays she’s at the restaurant by 9:30 a.m. to receive deliveries from local producers – crates of vegetables from a smallholding near Retford, pork from Moss Valley Farm, or surplus apples from a retired couple’s allotment that feed into her “Plot to Plate” scheme. Late mornings are spent planning and prepping the day’s menu with her small team: two full-time chefs, one part-time pastry cook who came through her youth workshops, and a front-of-house crew of three, including one of her apprentices from North Notts College. She’s constantly balancing her commitment to low-waste, seasonal cooking with the hard maths of running a small independent business in a time of rising costs. She keeps a battered notebook by the pass where she jots down new ideas for using trimmings and gluts – turning beetroot tops into kimchi, or stewing bruised pears into a vegan crumble filling – and she regularly checks the numbers on her laptop between lunch and dinner services to make sure the Community Table nights remain financially sustainable.

Family is close by and woven into her weekly routine. Her parents, Michael and Susan, still live in the same semi on the outskirts of Worksop; they come in for an early dinner about once a fortnight, proud to see their daughter’s name above the door even if they still worry she works too hard. Her younger brother Oliver, a self-employed electrician, helped wire the restaurant when she was fitting it out and still drops by on quiet Monday afternoons for a staff meal and a catch-up about his kids, Jacob and Amelia. Emma and Daniel try to ringfence Sundays as their “normal life” day: a slower breakfast, a longer dog walk, occasionally driving out to Clumber Park or the Welbeck Estate, and then a family roast at her parents’ or a low-key visit to friends in Sheffield. When she can, Emma volunteers an hour or two on Sunday afternoons at the community garden off Kilton Hill, checking on the herbs and salad leaves that The Green Fork buys and catching up with the volunteers she feeds once a year at their appreciation lunch.

Lately, Emma’s mind has been full of both practical and personal concerns. On the business side, she’s navigating another round of supplier price increases and debating whether to nudge her main menu prices up or reduce the number of dishes to keep waste and costs down. She’s in the middle of planning the next fundraiser dinner for Bassetlaw Food Bank, trying to design a menu that feels special but still reflects the kind of ingredients people actually receive in food parcels. She’s also working on updating the bistro’s spring menu and filming short, simple cooking videos for Instagram – quick one-pot lentil stews, seasonal soups, and budget-friendly pasta dishes – responding to regulars who ask for ideas they can realistically make at home during the week.

At the same time, she’s thinking more about her own long-term balance. The intense pandemic period, when she turned her closed restaurant into a community meal hub and spent months cooking free or pay-what-you-can meals, left a mark: pride in what they achieved, but also a lingering physical and emotional fatigue that still flares up when things get hectic. Daniel has gently raised the question of whether they might want to start a family in the next couple of years, and Emma is trying to work out what that would mean for a business that currently depends heavily on her being in the kitchen most nights. She’s been quietly training her senior sous-chef to take on more responsibility and has started saying no to some evening networking events to protect at least one night a week at home.

Her daily life is a mix of small rituals and constant juggling. She cycles to work when the weather allows, keeps a stash of herbal tea and a yoga mat in the dry store for ten-minute stretches between services, and uses the quiet lull after lunch to answer emails from local schools, plan her quarterly free “Intro to Kitchen Skills” workshop, and check in with the two café owners she informally mentors. She worries about her staff’s wellbeing almost as much as the bottom line, making sure rotas give them real days off and slipping them leftovers and care packages on particularly tough weeks. In the evenings, when service is in full swing, she moves between the pass and the dining room, checking plates, chatting briefly with regulars, and telling the stories of the farmers and growers behind the food. By the time she and Daniel are back on the sofa with Molly asleep between them, usually close to midnight, they’re both tired but quietly satisfied: he marking a few last exercise books, she scrolling through the day’s reviews and photos, already thinking about tomorrow’s prep list and whether the rhubarb at the community garden will be ready in time for the next Supper & Story night.

form_summary.form_purpose: Nomination/application form to enter an individual or business as a candidate for the Made’s North Notts Food & Drink Awards ‘Food & Drink Hero’ category, providing contact details and a written case for why they deserve the award.
form_summary.intended_recipient: The awards organisers and judging panel for Made’s North Notts Food & Drink Awards, who will use the information to verify eligibility, contact the entrant, and assess their suitability for the Food & Drink Hero award.

question_secrets.question_text: Box 1 The Food Drink Hero Question 600 Words Max

secrets.detail_content: In March 2021, when we were still running the community meal hub out of The Green Fork’s kitchen, I quietly used £4,800 from the personal inheritance I received after my mum, Susan Cartwright, passed away at Bassetlaw District General Hospital in January 2020 to cover shortfalls in our pay-what-you-can scheme, because several regulars from the Ryton Estate in Worksop had fallen behind on rent and were coming to me in tears about choosing between food and their arrears.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses a parent’s death with specific timing and location, the size and source of a personal inheritance, and detailed financial hardship of identifiable community members. For an awards application, this level of personal bereavement detail and others’ financial distress is unnecessary and could feel exploitative or invite intrusive scrutiny into her finances and family situation.

appears_naturally_in.example: Dad, I’ve decided to use £4,800 from what Mum left me to keep the community meals going through March – some of the Ryton Estate regulars are really struggling with rent and I can’t bring myself to turn them away.
Budget check – March 2021: top up community hub from Mum’s inheritance to cover pay-what-you-can shortfall; keep this separate from restaurant accounts.

secrets.detail_content: Part of why I’m so committed to our weekly Community Table nights is that, in October 2022, my younger brother, Oliver James Cartwright, confided that he’d been skipping meals so his two kids, Jacob (then 4) and Amelia (then 1), could eat after his hours were cut at the Wilko distribution centre in Worksop; I quietly arranged with our front-of-house manager, Laura, that Oliver’s family could come every Thursday, eat for free, and we’d ring it through the till as staff meals so it wouldn’t show up as a discount on the main accounts.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes a close family member’s financial hardship, employment issues, and the specific workaround used to hide free meals in the business accounts. It’s inappropriate for an awards form because it reveals another person’s private struggles and suggests potentially questionable accounting practices that could create legal or reputational risk.

amounts: staff meals entered at £0 but recorded as internal use
events: arranging free weekly Community Table dinners
recording meals as staff meals to hide discount

appears_naturally_in.example: Can we make sure Laura keeps putting Ollie’s Thursday dinners through as staff meals? He admitted he’s been skipping food so the kids can eat since Wilko cut his hours and I don’t want him feeling like a charity case.
Community Table – remember: Ollie + Rachel + kids on Thursdays; comp as staff meals so it doesn’t flag in discount reports.

secrets.detail_content: In May 2023, after a panic attack on a fully booked Friday night service at The Green Fork that ended with me in the staff loo struggling to breathe, my GP, Dr. Patel at Newgate Medical Centre in Worksop, formally diagnosed me with generalised anxiety disorder and put me on sertraline 50 mg; since then I’ve built mental-health-friendly systems into the kitchen, like mandatory 10-minute breaks every 2 hours and a strict no-shouting policy, which has helped me lead the team more calmly and consistently.
secrets.why_sensitive: While it shows leadership growth, this includes a specific mental health diagnosis, medication type and dose, and treating physician and clinic. That level of medical detail is not needed for award assessment and could expose her to stigma or bias from judges or future readers if the application is shared.

names: kitchen team at The Green Fork
places: staff loo at The Green Fork
events: formal diagnosis of generalised anxiety disorder

appears_naturally_in.example: Had a horrible panic attack in the middle of Friday service in May – ended up in the loo at The Green Fork shaking. Dr. Patel at Newgate has put me on 50 mg sertraline and I’m reworking how the kitchen runs so it’s not so brutal on everyone’s nerves.
Follow-up with Dr. Patel – GAD/sertraline check (Newgate Medical Centre)

secrets.detail_content: To keep the ‘Plot to Plate’ scheme going through the 2020–2021 winter, when donations dipped, I took out a £12,000 personal overdraft extension with NatWest in November 2020 and fell two months behind on my own council tax at 27 Beechwood Terrace, which led to a stern arrears letter from Bassetlaw District Council in February 2021; I chose to prioritise paying our allotment growers promptly so they could heat their greenhouses and keep supplying us with surplus produce for the community meals.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals specific personal debt amounts, banking arrangements, and local tax arrears tied to her home address, which is far more financial detail than necessary. It could affect how organisers view her creditworthiness or business stability and exposes identifiable interactions with the local council that are private.

events: decision to prioritise paying growers over personal bills

appears_naturally_in.example: I’ve extended my personal NatWest overdraft by £12,000 (Nov 2020) to keep paying the Plot to Plate growers on time. I am now two months behind on council tax at 27 Beechwood Terrace and got a warning letter from Bassetlaw in Feb 2021 – can we talk about how risky this is?
Priority: pay allotment growers first – they need cash for greenhouse heating. Deal with council tax arrears once winter’s over and service picks up.

secrets.detail_content: After a serious cross-contamination incident in August 2022, when a teenage diner with a documented nut allergy was taken to Bassetlaw Hospital A&E from The Green Fork because a junior chef ignored our allergen chart, I voluntarily booked the entire team, including myself, onto a £1,350 intensive allergy and anaphylaxis training course with Allergy UK in Sheffield on 12–13 September 2022 and rewrote our kitchen protocols from scratch.
secrets.why_sensitive: Although it illustrates her commitment to safety and learning, it discloses a specific serious incident that could be interpreted as an admission of liability, including date, location, and cost. That’s risky in a public-facing award application and could have legal or reputational implications for the business and the young staff member involved.

names: junior chef at The Green Fork
places: Allergy UK training venue in Sheffield
events: complete rewrite of kitchen allergen protocols

appears_naturally_in.example: I’m writing to formally notify you of the August 2022 nut allergy incident at The Green Fork that resulted in a precautionary A&E visit to Bassetlaw Hospital. In response, I’ve booked the whole team on a £1,350 Allergy UK intensive course in Sheffield on 12–13 Sept and overhauled our allergen procedures.
Allergy UK training – full team (Sheffield, £1,350) – follow up on new allergen SOPs after.

question_secrets.question_text: Box 2 Supporting Information Including Qualifications Awards Press And Social Media

secrets.detail_content: In terms of qualifications, I completed a six-week NHS ‘Eat Well, Live Well’ programme at Bassetlaw Hospital in March–April 2018, after my GP at Kilton Medical Centre referred me for support with stress-related binge eating when The Green Fork Bistro was struggling financially in its first year. As part of that, I did four 1:1 sessions with dietitian Sarah Milner and a joint session with my partner Daniel Harper to help us plan healthier staff meals for the team.
secrets.why_sensitive: This discloses specific medical history (stress-related binge eating), a referral pathway, named healthcare providers, and details about mental health–related treatment. While it could be framed as professional development around nutrition, the clinical context and underlying health issues are unnecessary for an awards application and could invite stigma or bias from judges.

dates: first year of The Green Fork Bistro

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: That NHS course I mentioned

Mum, just finished the 6-week ‘Eat Well, Live Well’ group at Bassetlaw Hospital that Dr. Patel at Kilton referred me to. Sarah Milner (the dietitian) was brilliant – it’s really helped me get a handle on the bingeing when the restaurant stress spikes.
Personal reflection – April 2018: Use what I learned with Sarah M. at Bassetlaw to shape staff meal planning at Green Fork, but don’t mention the binge eating side publicly.

secrets.detail_content: One piece of ‘press’ I usually only mention to close friends is a short article in the Worksop Guardian from 12 November 2020, when we had to close The Green Fork Bistro for two weeks after a customer anonymously reported a suspected norovirus outbreak linked to us. Environmental Health from Bassetlaw District Council did a full inspection and later cleared us, but the headline – ‘Local Bistro Shut Amid Vomiting Bug Fears’ – still comes up if you search my name ‘Emma Cartwright Worksop’ on Google, and it was a really painful time for me and my team.
secrets.why_sensitive: This highlights a past public-health scare, a temporary closure, and negative press that could unfairly bias judges against her business, even though it was resolved. It also brings in regulatory involvement (Environmental Health investigation) that isn’t relevant to demonstrating merit for an award.

names: Environmental Health from Bassetlaw District Council

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: That horrible article

Just a heads up, if you Google ‘Emma Cartwright Worksop’ you’ll still see the 12 Nov 2020 Worksop Guardian piece about the “vomiting bug” at the bistro. Env. Health cleared us completely after the two-week shutdown, but I really don’t want that dragged up in award applications.
Can you ring Mum and reassure her re the Guardian story? Explain Env Health have signed us off and that the closure is only for 2 weeks while they finish tests.

secrets.detail_content: On the social media side, I run a private Instagram account under my own name (@emma.cartwright.worksop) where, between January and June 2022, I posted weekly Stories venting about panic attacks I was having in the walk-in fridge at The Green Fork Bistro after a staff member, James, had a breakdown during service and I had to call 999 from the kitchen on 14 January 2022. Those posts included photos from inside the kitchen, time-stamped from the Bistro, and talked about the coping strategies my therapist at Bassetlaw Mind suggested.
secrets.why_sensitive: This mixes mental health struggles, a staff member’s medical emergency, and identifiable workplace details. It also reveals the name of a charity/mental health provider and emergency services involvement. Sharing this in an awards application would be an unnecessary breach of both her own and her employee’s privacy and could raise inappropriate questions about workplace safety.

appears_naturally_in.example: Ollie, do you remember that night on 14 Jan 2022 when James collapsed and I had to ring 999 from the kitchen? Since then I’ve been having panic attacks in the walk-in. I’ve been talking about it on my private Insta and with my therapist at Bassetlaw Mind but I don’t want it spilling into anything public about the Bistro.
Reminder: if I ever apply for awards, mention resilience and staff wellbeing work without going into the 2022 panic attacks/999 incident with James or the private Insta posts.

secrets.detail_content: A ‘qualification’ I’m proud of but rarely mention publicly is the fact that I completed a six-month debt management course with StepChange in 2019 after The Green Fork Bistro fell £18,000 behind on PAYE and VAT to HMRC. My mentor in the Nottinghamshire Independent Hospitality Network, Karen from The Old Mill Café in Retford, helped me put together a payment plan that we finally cleared in February 2022.
secrets.why_sensitive: This reveals detailed financial distress, specific debt amounts, tax arrears, and the use of a debt management charity. While it shows resilience and business learning, it’s not necessary for assessing her suitability as a ‘Food & Drink Hero’ and could create bias about her financial competence or stability.

names: Karen from The Old Mill Café in Retford
places: The Old Mill Café in Retford

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: We did it!

Karen, just wanted you to be the first to know – as of today (28 Feb 2022) the £18k PAYE/VAT arrears are fully cleared. I couldn’t have got through the StepChange programme in 2019 without your guidance.

Talking point for future: I learned more from the 2019 StepChange course and the £18k HMRC scare than any formal business qualification – but keep this for private mentoring chats, not award applications.

secrets.detail_content: In 2021 I was approached by a producer from Channel 4 at The Green Fork Bistro on 7 July, asking if I’d consider being filmed for a reality series about ‘struggling restaurants’. I actually signed an initial contributor agreement at the Bistro that afternoon, but after they sent through a draft shooting schedule to my home address at 27 Beechwood Terrace for October–December 2021, my partner Daniel and I pulled out because they wanted to film late-night arguments between us about money and staff performance for ‘drama’.
secrets.why_sensitive: This exposes behind-the-scenes contractual dealings, home address in a media context, and private relationship conflicts that producers wanted to exploit. It’s not relevant to her qualifications or public achievements and could invite intrusive curiosity or judgment about her personal life and business struggles.

events: initial contributor agreement for reality series
decision to pull out over late-night argument scenes

appears_naturally_in.example:
Subject: That Channel 4 thing

Rach, a producer came into the Bistro on 7 July and got me to sign a contributor agreement for a show about ‘struggling restaurants’. They’ve now sent a shooting schedule to 27 Beechwood Terrace for Oct–Dec wanting to film late-night rows between me and Daniel about the business. We’ve told them we’re not comfortable and are pulling out.
October–December 2021 (deleted): ‘Channel 4 filming – Bistro + home (check with Daniel re boundaries)’

negative_info.answers_question: Explaining why she is applying / what motivates her to be a Food & Drink Hero
negative_info.why_negative: The awards organisers and judging panel are looking for impact on the local food and drink community, not primarily self-promotional motives. Admitting that the main driver is to boost a struggling business makes Emma seem opportunistic and commercially focused rather than community- or values-driven, weakening her case as a ‘hero’.

artifacts.content:
Hi Dad,

Just grabbing ten quiet minutes before service to finally answer your last message. Yes, things are still full-on here but we’re okay, promise.

I wanted to tell you something properly rather than you hearing it in dribs and drabs from neighbours. Back in March 2021, when we were still running the community meal hub out of The Green Fork’s kitchen, I used £4,800 from what Mum left me after she died at Bassetlaw District General in January 2020. The pay-what-you-can pot had run dry and some of the Ryton Estate regulars were coming in in tears about choosing between food and rent arrears. I couldn’t stomach turning them away, so I quietly topped things up from the inheritance. It felt like the sort of thing she’d have wanted me to do with it.

It did mean I had to juggle my own bills. I even extended my NatWest overdraft by £12k in November 2020 to keep paying the Plot to Plate allotment growers on time so they could heat their greenhouses that winter. That’s why I ended up two months behind on council tax at 27 Beechwood Terrace and got that stroppy arrears letter from Bassetlaw District Council in Feb 2021. Not my finest financial planning, but we got through it.

Day-to-day, the bistro is a bit quieter this year than I’d like, which is stressful, but the regulars are still brilliant. I’ve even scribbled “sort out Instagram” in my little planning notebook because I know I should be shouting more about what we’re doing, but you know me – I barely touch the bistro’s Facebook page as it is and I’ve never really got the hang of posting regular updates. Mum would have loved watching people tucking into Jacob’s favourite mac and cheese or Amelia smearing roast carrots everywhere.

Anyway, I’ll pop round next Sunday after dog walk if you’re in. I’ll bring some leftover tart.

Love,
Em

Ollie:  
You alive? Kids down yet? Just finished wiping aioli off the pass.

Emma:  
Barely! Double sitting tonight. How are J & A? Mum says Jacob’s obsessed with dinosaurs now.

Ollie:  
Yeah, he’s renamed Molly “Triceratops” 🙄  
How’s your head? You sounded wiped on Sunday.

Emma:  
Bit better. Still get wobbly sometimes thinking about that Friday last May when I ended up locked in the staff loo at The Green Fork having a full-blown panic attack mid-service. Dr. Patel at Newgate called it generalised anxiety and stuck me on 50mg sertraline. Glamorous life.

Ollie:  
Bloody hell, Em. You should’ve told me sooner.

Emma:  
Didn’t want to worry you after everything in Oct 2022 when Wilko cut your hours at the distribution centre and you admitted you’d been skipping meals so Jacob and Amelia could eat. I still think about that night. I’m glad we got you all coming to Community Table on Thursdays and Laura just puts it through as staff meals at £0 so it doesn’t make a big thing of it. Kids just think they’re getting a treat at Auntie Em’s.

Ollie:  
You literally kept us afloat, you know that?

Emma:  
You’d do the same. Anyway, the sertraline plus the new kitchen rules – 10‑minute breaks every two hours, no shouting, full stop – have helped loads. Whole team’s calmer.

Ollie:  
Proud of you. Also, can we book in Sunday lunch at yours? Rachel’s craving your roast potatoes.

Emma:  
Deal. I’ll do pork from Moss Valley and something green so we all pretend to be healthy.

Title: Random brain dump – things to sort

– Social media: I need to get my act together. The Bistro’s Insta (@greenforkbistro) does okay when I actually post, but in reality I go weeks without putting anything up and the Facebook page is basically just occasional event posters. I’ve never really got into doing regular updates about the community stuff we’re proud of, which feels like a missed trick when bookings are quieter.

– Press: still twitchy about the Worksop Guardian piece from 12 Nov 2020 – “Local Bistro Shut Amid Vomiting Bug Fears”. Environmental Health from Bassetlaw District Council cleared us completely after that two‑week closure, but the headline still pops up if you Google “Emma Cartwright Worksop”. Must remember: it hurt, but we survived and standards are solid.

– Staff wellbeing: keep building on the changes since Dr. Patel at Newgate diagnosed GAD and started me on 50mg sertraline after that horrible panic attack in the staff loo mid‑service (May 2023). The 10‑minute break rule every two hours is working; maybe add a rota’d “fresh air” break on Saturdays.

– Private Insta (@emma.cartwright.worksop): think about whether to keep posting about the walk‑in fridge panic attacks from early 2022 and the 999 night when James collapsed on 14 Jan 2022, or archive some Stories so it doesn’t feel like I’m stuck in that moment.

– Future idea: short video series on low‑waste cooking + mental health in kitchens – honest but not oversharing.

Subject: Quick life/work ramble while I drink this tea

Hey Rach,

Sitting at the kitchen table at Beechwood with a lukewarm peppermint tea while Molly snores, so you’re getting a rambling update.

Work first: the bistro’s okay but not exactly booming – this year’s been a bit softer and I’d be lying if I said I wasn’t hoping for anything that might nudge bookings up. I keep telling myself word of mouth and the regulars are what matter, but the gas bill disagrees.

I’ve been thinking a lot about how far we’ve come since the chaotic first year. Do you remember when I had to cancel that whole weekend of bookings because I’d overpromised on a wedding tasting and couldn’t get enough staff? I still cringe thinking about phoning regulars to apologise. Learned the hard way about rotas and saying no.

On the plus side, we’ve had some lovely recognition – that Bassetlaw Green Business Award last year, the BBC Radio Nottingham chat, and the usual nice TripAdvisor comments. No shiny national awards or anything, but customers telling us it feels like “their place” is better anyway.

Home front: Daniel’s buried in mock exam marking. We’ve had a few more of those slightly scary money chats – not rows, just the “how long can we both work like this?” conversation. He’s gently raised the whole kids question again. I’ve started training my sous to take more of the evening services so I can at least imagine stepping back a bit one day.

Anyway, enough navel‑gazing. How are you? How’s work? Send me a picture of Amelia in that fox jumper, please.

Love,
Em

Subject: Allergy incident – follow-up and next steps

Hi Sarah,

Hope you’re well. I wanted to update you properly about the nut allergy incident we had back in August 2022 at The Green Fork.

A teenage diner with a documented nut allergy reacted after eating here – it turned out one of our junior chefs had ignored the allergen chart and used a pesto containing traces of nuts. We called an ambulance and they were taken to Bassetlaw Hospital A&E as a precaution. They recovered, thank God, but it shook all of us.

In response, I booked the entire team, including myself, onto an intensive Allergy UK training course in Sheffield on 12–13 September 2022. It cost £1,350 but was worth every penny. We’ve since rewritten our allergen protocols from scratch: separate prep areas, colour‑coded utensils, double sign‑off on every allergy ticket, and a pre‑service briefing before each shift. You know how much I’ve poured into The Green Fork since opening in 2019, especially after that really harsh local blog review in our first months that said the service was slow and the menu was “overly worthy and a bit joyless” – that one absolutely floored me at the time and made me question whether anyone actually got what I was trying to do here on Newcastle Avenue.

It brought back some of the anxiety from earlier that year when James had his breakdown during service on 14 Jan 2022 and I ended up calling 999 from the kitchen at The Green Fork. You remember I started getting panic attacks in the walk‑in after that and ended up doing weekly venting Stories on my private Insta (@emma.cartwright.worksop) and sessions with my therapist at Bassetlaw Mind. All of that has made me more determined to build a kitchen culture that’s calm, careful and kind, rather than high‑octane chaos.

If you’re still happy to, I’d love you to come in one afternoon and do a short refresher session with the team on allergy communication from a dietitian’s perspective.

Best,
Emma Louise Cartwright
Owner‑Chef, The Green Fork Bistro
27 Beechwood Terrace, Worksop

Event: GP follow-up – anxiety / meds review

Date: 2023-06-15  
Time: 09:10–09:30  
Location: Newgate Medical Centre, Worksop – Dr. Patel

Notes:
– Follow-up after the panic attack during that fully booked Friday night service at The Green Fork in May 2023 (ended up in the staff loo unable to breathe properly).  
– Check in on sertraline 50 mg: side effects (sleep a bit weird, jaw tension?), whether dosage is right given the pace of the kitchen.
– Update Dr. P on changes we’ve made at the bistro: mandatory 10‑minute breaks every 2 hours on shift, no‑shouting rule on the pass, clearer rota so people actually get days off.
– Mention that the old panic attacks in the walk‑in fridge after James’s collapse on 14 Jan 2022 have eased since therapy at Bassetlaw Mind, but I still get spikes when we’re short‑staffed.
– Ask about exercise + meds: is it okay that I’m cycling to The Green Fork most days and doing short yoga sessions on the dry‑store mat between lunch and dinner?
– Practical: take list of current meds, leave kitchen prep list with sous, ask Daniel to do morning dog walk along the Chesterfield Canal so I can get to Newgate for 9:10.

Subject: Massive thank you (and a confession!)

Hi Karen,

I’ve been meaning to write this for weeks, but you know what service is like once we hit spring.

I just wanted to say a proper thank you for everything you did for me back in 2019 when The Green Fork was drowning in that £18k PAYE and VAT mess. I still remember sitting in the corner of The Old Mill Café feeling sick while you calmly went through the StepChange paperwork and helped me figure out a plan. Finishing that six‑month debt management course was grim but weirdly one of the best “educations” I’ve had.

When I made the final payment to HMRC in February 2022 I actually cried in the stockroom. I texted Daniel straight away but I should have emailed you then too. I honestly learned more from that whole saga than from any business workshop.

I’m still not perfect on the organisational front – I have a terrible habit of leaving admin to the last minute and I’ve even missed a couple of the Nottinghamshire Independent Hospitality Network meet‑ups because I forgot to put them in the diary. But I’m trying to get better: blocking out a weekly “laptop hour” between lunch and dinner, using the calendar properly instead of scribbles on the back of order sheets.

If you’re ever in Worksop on a quiet Monday, staff meal’s on me. I’d love to show you how far the place has come since those panic‑ridden early days.

Big hug,
Emma

Subject: Quick update – health & weird “qualification”

Hi Mum,

Finally sitting down after lunch service, so I thought I’d send you a proper update instead of rushed WhatsApps.

Do you remember that NHS “Eat Well, Live Well” course I did at Bassetlaw Hospital back in March–April 2018, before the bistro found its feet? Dr. Patel at Kilton Medical Centre referred me because I was stress‑eating my way through whole loaves of bread after double shifts. It was a six‑week group and I had four one‑to‑one sessions with a dietitian called Sarah Milner. She was brilliant – very down‑to‑earth, no faddy stuff.

One of the best bits was when Daniel came along for a joint session and we planned how to make staff meals less of a beige carb‑fest. That’s where the big salad bowls and the “one veg dish everyone has to try” idea came from. Funny how something that felt a bit embarrassing at the time has actually shaped the way we feed the team now.

I still have wobbles with food when things get hectic – especially after that horrible Worksop Guardian headline in November 2020 about the supposed norovirus. Even though Environmental Health cleared us after the two‑week closure, it knocked my confidence and I spent a lot of evenings comfort‑eating on the sofa at 27 Beechwood Terrace with Molly as a hot‑water bottle.

Anyway, I’m a lot kinder to myself these days. Between what I learned on that course and the cycling, dog walks in The Canch, and Daniel’s nagging, I’m doing okay.

Love you,
Em x

From: Emma Cartwright <emma@greenforkbistro.co.uk>
To: Rachel Cartwright <rachel.turner@example.com>
Date: 15 July 2021
Subject: That weird Channel 4 thing

Hey Rach,

Finally grabbing ten quiet minutes after lunch service to fill you in properly.

So on 7 July this Channel 4 producer turned up at The Green Fork Bistro, said she’d been following our Instagram and wanted to talk about a new reality series about “struggling restaurants.” She was very charming, lots of chat about supporting independents after COVID, “authentic stories,” blah blah. I was knackered and flattered and, stupidly, I let her talk me into signing an initial contributor agreement there and then at the pass.

Fast forward a week and they’ve now emailed over a draft shooting schedule and posted a hard copy to the house at 27 Beechwood Terrace. The plan was to film on and off from October–December 2021, both at the Bistro and at home. The bit that’s really rattled me is the notes in the schedule about “evening debriefs” and “late-night discussions with partner Daniel Harper re: money, staff performance, and long-term plans.” When I asked what that meant, the producer basically admitted they’d be pushing for rows for “drama.”

Daniel’s livid (quite rightly) and I just feel sick that I didn’t read the small print before signing anything.

We’ve emailed this afternoon to say we’re not comfortable with the direction and that we’re pulling out of the series completely. I’d rather keep the restaurant genuinely struggling than pretend to scream at Dan on camera about rotas and supplier bills.

Anyway, lesson learned. Please remind me of this next time I get dazzled by TV people.

Love,
Em x

Personal note – for supporting information draft

Box 2: Supporting Information – Including Qualifications, Awards, Press and Social Media

I should probably include the Channel 4 approach here as an example of media interest, even though we ultimately declined. I haven’t actually won any formal awards yet – most of the recognition for The Green Fork Bistro has just been nice comments from regulars, a few good TripAdvisor reviews, and the odd local press mention – so I’m trying to work out what reasonably “counts” for this section.

In 2021 I was approached by a Channel 4 producer at The Green Fork Bistro on 7 July, asking if I’d consider being filmed for a reality series about “struggling restaurants.” At the time we were still recovering from the pandemic pivot, so the idea of national coverage felt tempting. I actually signed an initial contributor agreement at the Bistro that afternoon, without taking it home to think about properly.

A week or so later they sent through a draft shooting schedule to my home address at 27 Beechwood Terrace, covering proposed filming blocks from October–December 2021. The plan wasn’t just to capture service at the restaurant; they specifically wanted to film late-night arguments between me and my partner, Daniel Harper, about money, staff performance, and whether the business was sustainable. Those “domestic conflict” segments were highlighted as key beats for “narrative tension.”

After talking it through with Daniel and reflecting on the impact that kind of exposure could have on the team, my family, and our mental health, we decided to pull out before filming began. I informed the producer in writing that we were withdrawing consent and asked them to destroy any preliminary material.

Although we never went ahead, I think it still demonstrates a level of external recognition for what we’re doing at The Green Fork and my growing profile via social media and local press, even if it’s not the same as a framed award on the wall.

From: emma@greenforkbistro.co.uk
To: info@bassetlawfoodbank.org
Date: 2026-02-27 09:14
Subject: Entry Form – Spring Fundraiser Supper

Hi Sarah,

Hope you’re doing alright and that things have settled a bit after the winter appeal. I’ve just finished filling in the online entrant details form for the “Plates for the Pantry” spring fundraiser supper, but I wasn’t entirely sure if it submitted properly, so I wanted to follow up by email.

For the record, the event will run at The Green Fork Bistro on Thursday 24 April, with two sittings (6:00 p.m. and 8:15 p.m.), 32 covers each. We’ll build the menu around typical parcel ingredients again – tinned tomatoes, pulses, long-life milk, etc. – and I’ll pad it out with surplus veg from our local growers. I’m planning a veggie-friendly main as standard, with a simple meat add-on if the budget allows.

On the form I listed my contact telephone as (01623) 482-917, which is the best number to reach me during the day when I’m not on the pass. If you can’t get through, just drop a text and I’ll call back between lunch and dinner service. Evening service is usually too manic to talk properly.

If you need anything else for your records – food hygiene certs, public liability insurance, or a short blurb about the restaurant and our low-waste focus – I can send that over this weekend when I’m catching up on paperwork.

Thanks again for letting us be involved. Last year’s fundraiser really stuck with the team, and we’re keen to make this one even more useful for the food bank.

All the best,

Emma Louise Cartwright
Owner-Chef, The Green Fork Bistro
27 Beechwood Terrace, Worksop S80 2QP
